content: {"answer": "The provided search results converge on a broad finding but disagree on specifics. Most randomized trials and pooled analyses of IF (alternate-day fasting, 5:2, and continuous IER) against CER show SIMILAR weight loss when energy intake is matched [Source 1, 2, 7, 8, 9]. Harvie et al.'s foundational work (Source 9) reported equivalent IER and CER weight loss (LOCF analysis) with comparable confidence intervals. A 2-year real-world dietary setting (Source 7) likewise found ICR and CCR equivalent for modest weight loss. Body composition effects are more mixed: a Frontiers in Nutrition meta-analysis (Source 10) suggested iER tends to be superior to cER in body composition and circumference reduction, with mean weight loss 4.57 kg, but a separate 12-week RCT (Source 8) found improvements were similar between the two approaches. For time-restricted eating specifically, Lowe 2020 (Source 11) showed no significant between-group weight loss difference vs consistent meal timing (secondary outcomes also examined fat mass, lean mass, fasting insulin/glucose/HbA1c); Chow 2020 (Source 14) found TRE did not cause trunk or arm lean mass loss and saw non-significant effects on fat mass, lean mass, and visceral fat. One outlier (Source 5) claims IF achieves weight loss and body composition improvement whereas CR does not, but this contradicts the preponderance of SR evidence in Sources 1, 2, 7, 8, 9. Overall, the sources do NOT support that IF produces superior glycemic or cardiometabolic biomarker improvements beyond what energy balance explains; they most strongly support equivalence, with a smaller body of work suggesting modest body-composition advantages that require confirmation. Sources do not provide direct isolation data for fasting-vs-energy-balance separate from the results already cited.", "key_findings": ["Multiple SRs and RCTs find IF and CER produce similar weight loss under matched energy intake conditions (Sources 1, 2, 7, 8, 9).", "Lowe 2020 TREAT trial: no significant between-group weight loss difference for 16:8 TRE vs consistent meal timing (Source 11).", "Chow 2020: TRE did not cause trunk/arm lean mass loss; non-significant effects on fat mass, lean mass, visceral fat (Source 14).", "Frontiers in Nutrition meta-analysis suggested iER superior to cER for body composition with mean weight loss 4.57 kg (Source 10) \u2014 mixed with equivalence findings elsewhere.", "Outlier Gavin Publishers paper (Source 5) claims CR produced no weight loss, contradicting the preponderance of SR evidence."], "sources_used": ["https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9484493/", "https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35084574/", "https://www.mdpi.com/2072-6643/13/4/1195", "https://examine.com/research-feed/study/dV4WJ0/", "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3017674/", "https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/nutrition/articles/10.3389/fnut.2022.979618/full", "https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamainternalmedicine/fullarticle/2771095", "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7180107/", "https://www.gavinpublishers.com/article/view/intermittent-fasting-vs-continuous-caloric-restriction-for-weight-and-body-composition-changes-in-humans"]}
